notes.anchor: concede to Caesar what he demands
notes.note: Caesar's demand was to stand for the consulship of 48 BC in absentia while retaining his Gallic army and provinces until he could enter office, so as never to be a private citizen liable to prosecution; the Senate's hardliners wanted him to lay down his command first.

notes.anchor: when we were prorogating his command for the second five years
notes.note: The lex Pompeia Licinia of 55 BC renewed Caesar's Gallic command for a further five years; Cicero's point is that the optimates armed Caesar by these earlier concessions and have no standing to resist him only now.

notes.anchor: the bill that his candidacy might be entertained in his absence
notes.note: The law of the ten tribunes (52 BC), backed by Pompey, exempted Caesar from the rule requiring candidates to canvass in person at Rome; this ratio absentis is the legal hinge of the whole crisis.
